---
title: "Is anyone else building cells this way?"
description: "I build a platelet model with an AI agent in the loop. Here's how that works, the existing work it leans on, and what still trips it up."
author: "Steve Haigh"
date: "2026-06-16"
categories: [llms, research, modelling]
draft: true
---

*[Scaffold — fill in. Findings from a deep-research run (23 verified claims, 2 refuted) plus two follow-up fetches (Robin, Owkin) are cited inline. Tone to aim for: restrained — "this is what I did and the work it builds on," not "first ever." Credit existing work generously. Keep lab-book specifics out; the public `platelet-wcm` repo and its public docs are fine to link.]*

Most days my research runs in one loop: read a paper on platelet calcium signalling, pull the rate equations out of it, get an AI agent to turn them into simulation code, run it, and check the output against published experiments — same agent, same session, start to finish. I'm a software engineer by training, so the coding half feels unremarkable. The biology half does not. I got curious whether other people work like this — partly to learn from them, partly to credit the work I'm leaning on. The short version: the individual pieces are well out there, some peer-reviewed; running them as one in-silico loop, on a cell like the platelet, just seems less common. So this is a write-up of what I did and the work it builds on, not a claim to anything.

## The loop, concretely

*[Show a real day, not an abstraction. The four steps with public artifacts: (1) reading primary literature on the Ca²⁺ toolkit — IP3R / SERCA / PMCA / SOCE / calmodulin; (2) deriving the rate equations; (3) the agent writing or modifying a `process` in the wcEcoli-derived framework; (4) validating against Dolan & Diamond 2014 — the ±extracellular-Ca²⁺ comparison (`runPhase3.py`). Link the public repo `github.com/stevehaigh/platelet-wcm` (README + runscripts). The point to land: this is ONE integrated loop, not four separate tools bolted together — literature, maths, code, and validation share a context. That sharing is the thing worth describing.]*

## Why I went looking

*[The honest motivation. Coding-with-AI is second nature to me — see [Why I trust LLMs for code and don't for biology](../llms-code-vs-biology/). The biology half is where my trust is low and the verification burden is high, so doing it *through* an agent felt worth sanity-checking against what others do. Four things I wanted to find: is AI used to build mechanistic models? to mine the literature for parameters? does anyone run the whole loop? and who can realistically do this solo? Frame it as locating myself on a map and finding people to cite — not planting a flag.]*

## The pieces all exist

*[Intro: every individual slice of what I do is already occupied by published work — some peer-reviewed. This section is here to credit it. Then the four findings:]*

**Building mechanistic models with AI.** *[KinModGPT (Maeda & Kurata, IJMS 2023, PMC10138937) — "first method applying GPT models to kinetic modeling": natural language → Antimony → SBML, though structure only. SciGym (arXiv 2507.02083, NeurIPS 2025) — a benchmark where agents build and repair SBML models across 350 BioModels. MCP-orchestrated multiscale modelling (BSC, bioRxiv 2025.09.10.675105) — an agent with 60+ MCP tools builds a cancer cell-fate model by natural language, "data collection to simulation setup." Talk2Biomodels (BMC Bioinformatics 2025, PMC12625589) — interacts with *existing* models but, by its authors' own words, "lacks functionality for expanding these models." Honest read: model construction from scratch is the least-served part of this cluster, and it happens to be the part I lean on most.]*

**Mining the literature for parameters — and catching errors.** *[This is the most active area. EnzyExtract (Wei et al., Protein Science 2025, PMC12355964): parsed 137,892 papers AND audited BRENDA, catching six entries with misassigned units or misread numbers during prior curation. That's essentially my "I found a transcription error in a published model's constants" story — but at scale and peer-reviewed, so I should point to it rather than dress mine up as special. Enzyme Co-Scientist (bioRxiv 2025.03.03.641178) — 91k+ kinetic entries from ~3,500 papers. zERExtractor (arXiv 2508.09995) — similar. What's different in my case is only that the catch happened *in the loop*, on a model I was actively building. Link the public write-up `reports/design/purvis-2008-k3-transcription-error.md` and let the artifact speak for itself.]*

**The whole loop, end to end.** *[Plenty of people are doing AI-led end-to-end science — just aimed at different targets than a from-literature, in-silico mechanistic model. Credit them properly: FutureHouse's **Robin** (multi-agent system orchestrating Crow/Falcon/Finch) ran hypothesis → experiment design → data analysis → follow-up and surfaced ripasudil for dry AMD — published in *Nature*, May 2026 — but it's therapeutic discovery with humans running the wet-lab work. The King lab / Chalmers loop (bioRxiv 2025.06.24.661378) closes on physical robotics — automated yeast cell culture + metabolomics — not ODE construction. The MCP/BSC loop spans data → simulation setup but doesn't read literature or validate. So the in-silico chain I run (read literature → derive rate equations → write simulation code → validate against published datasets) overlaps with all of these in spirit and none of them exactly. Describe that honestly, as "near neighbours," not "no one's done it."]*

**Who can do this solo.** *[The democratization idea is articulated mostly in tool papers — Bhatnagar (arXiv 2509.07283) says an agentic ODE-calibration workflow "lowers the barrier… abstracts away much of the multidisciplinary expertise traditionally required… without sacrificing rigor or expert oversight." That's the vision stated by tool-builders. I haven't found many first-person accounts of a solo, M.Res-scale researcher actually running the whole mechanistic loop day to day — so if mine is useful at all, it's as one such data point, offered modestly.]*

## How this fits in

*[Restrained placement, not a novelty claim. State plainly what I'm NOT claiming: not the first to use AI for mechanistic modelling (KinModGPT, SciGym, MCP/BSC), not the first to mine or audit the literature with AI (EnzyExtract, peer-reviewed), not a new method. What I AM doing: running the existing pieces together as one in-silico loop, on a system (platelet calcium signalling) I haven't seen it applied to. Owkin's "vibe research for biology" essay (June 2025) gives a handy label — "AI-led with human oversight," distinct from human-led and AI-assisted — and points at the same broader wave (Google's AI co-scientist, Stanford's Biomni); my workflow is one narrow, human-verified instance of that idea rather than anything grander. On platelets specifically: I haven't found AI-assisted mechanistic modelling in platelet / calcium-signalling biology, but I've not run a systematic search — if it exists I'd genuinely like to cite it, so treat that as an open invitation rather than a "nobody has."]*

## Where it breaks

*[Foreground the failure modes — this is what keeps the post honest, and it's probably the most useful part for anyone tempted to try the same. SciGym: every frontier model's performance "declined significantly as system complexity increased," and agents "overfit to specific experimental data." LMR-Bench (arXiv 2506.17335, EMNLP 2025): best autonomous reproduction was ~43% unit-test accuracy, and full agent mode did *worse* than plain prompting — but state the nuance fairly: under LLM-as-judge the agents produced more functions judged correct; the shortfall traced to repository dependency handling, not reasoning. So don't say "agents are just worse." Two things I must NOT claim (both refuted in verification): that LLMs "can't set parameters automatically," and that the Novadiscovery PDF-to-SBML case reproduced its model successfully. The real burden in my workflow: every equation and constant the agent proposes gets checked against the source. Verification is the bottleneck, and I'm the verifier — the agent compressed the mechanical work and left me doing more judgement, faster.]*

## Why I'm sharing this

*[Not "here's a first" — just that a concrete, public, honestly-flawed worked example might help someone trying the same thing. The tool papers describe the vision; this is one person's narrow, day-to-day practice, with real artifacts (the repo, the validation runs against Dolan & Diamond, the caught error) and the rough edges left in. If there's a takeaway, it's the verification-is-the-job point: the AI didn't replace the science, it moved where my time goes. I hope it's interesting; corrections and "actually, X already did this" replies are welcome.]*

## Sources & further reading

*[Verified citations, grouped. Models: KinModGPT (IJMS 2023, PMC10138937); SciGym (arXiv 2507.02083); MCP/BSC multiscale (bioRxiv 2025.09.10.675105); agentic ODE calibration (arXiv 2509.07283); Talk2Biomodels (BMC Bioinformatics 2025, PMC12625589). Literature work: EnzyExtract (Protein Science 2025, PMC12355964); Enzyme Co-Scientist (bioRxiv 2025.03.03.641178); zERExtractor (arXiv 2508.09995). End-to-end / adjacent: FutureHouse "Robin" (multi-agent therapeutic discovery; ripasudil for dry AMD, Nature 2026); King lab wet-lab loop (bioRxiv 2025.06.24.661378); Owkin "vibe research for biology" essay (vendor vision piece, June 2025, coins "AI-led with human oversight"; cites Google AI co-scientist + Stanford Biomni). Failure modes: SciGym (2507.02083); LMR-Bench (arXiv 2506.17335). My own posts: [wcEcoli, briefly](../wcecoli-briefly/), [A platelet has no nucleus](../a-platelet-has-no-nucleus/), [Why I trust LLMs for code and don't for biology](../llms-code-vs-biology/). Notes: several sources are preprints, not peer-reviewed; the field moves fast (most cluster in 2025); benchmark numbers were measured on ~one-generation-old models. Internal links assume these posts sit alongside this one — check them when this moves out of `drafts/`.]*
